sentences.text: Right.
It took the army to settle in, the division settled in.
The first day when they came, they were separating the sick from the well ones.
I was already considered a well person by then.
But others needed the room in that shift-made hospital that they had-- makeshift hospital.
And whoever needed care, medical care, was given.
They were very gentle and very generous in that respect.
I was looking for a pair of shoes.
I'm very honest.
I had no shoes to wear.
So I found a pair of slippers which I adopted for myself.
Later on, I received a pair of shoes from the army, the American Army.
But meanwhile, I had at least a pair of shoes without holes in them to wear.
And it was an interesting town.
And I went to look at those steps.
I don't recall the name of it.
It was a little-- I guess maybe town of Mauthausen.
I don't know.
Because it looked like this was the barracks for the guards, or something like that, brick little houses, but nobody was around, not a soul.
I never saw anything as deserted as that.
And a few days later, we were given-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were taken to the barracks that were in camp, in Mauthausen itself in the camp.
By the gates, there were a few barracks built, brick buildings.
And we were given those quarters out of that gypsy camp and out of the floor.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I always thought I won't know how to sleep on a bed on a sheet, with a blanket.
But you know, I got accustomed very quickly to it.
It was the most lux-- first of all, it was the most luxurious feeling to be able to go into a shower, and wash yourself, and use that water to your heart's desire.
After all those months where you were watched, where you weren't allowed to spend an extra minute with a little bit of water that was given to you, you were not allowed.
But this was the most luxurious feeling to have that shower, to get washed.
Even it was cold water, but it was water, and then to lie down on a clean sheet on a bed.
And we were-- we shared the room, about two, four, about six girls shared one room.
And we had our private toilet like a sink and a toilet.
The showers were outside in the hallway.
But this was the most luxurious living after all those years that we had to undergo whatever deprivation we went through.
Now, the gypsy men-- there was a roundup, whoever was able to run out into the forest and grab the Nazis and whoever, and they brought them back to camp.
And a few of the gypsy men tried to do to them what they were doing to the-- to not only the Jewish people, but to any Haftling, anybody who was incarcerated, the routine.
And the American commandant did not like it.
He stopped it.
He stopped it, because he felt it's inhuman.
That the treatment that we were given was inhuman.
But we do not have to lose our humanity.
Maybe he was right.
I don't know.
I'm not going to be a judge of that.
I can't-- I couldn't judge then, and I'm not going to judge now.
Because, first of all, it's too late.
But I just-- first of all, I was not strong enough to do any-- any damage to anybody, even if I wanted to.
But I don't think that we can do that.
I don't think that we were brought up that way to harm anybody.
And I guess that's why we didn't do it.
We didn't participate.
That's why maybe we didn't insist on an immediate punishment.
Whatever punishment they had later on, I have no idea.
But they went to prison.
Whoever was round up went to prison.
This we know.
We had a choice to make.
After-- yes, and the care was given to us too, medical care, via glucose injection to bring us back to life, to give us some more stamina.
The food was brought in five times a day, a little bit at a time, and not heavy stuff, so whoever was able to come back to themselves.
And then sometimes in July-- we don't-- there was a treaty made with Russia that a certain part of Austria belonged to Russia, a certain part to whatever.
We had to evacuate.
We had a choice.
Either go back to our country, or go to a DP camp in deep Germany.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't savor either choice.

But I felt maybe by pure, sheer luck, if I went through years of hardship, years of deprivation, typhoid, two typhoids at the same time, sick like a dog, frozen feet-- maybe by pure, sheer luck, one of my sisters survived.
So I chose to go back to Cracow.
Good or bad, or in between, we don't going to discuss that.
But I went to Poland.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] many survivors did go back.
It was a mistake, maybe, I admit.
Polish people did not savor our presence.
While I was in Poland, there was a Kielce pogrom, there was a Radom pogrom.
There was-- people tried to smuggle themselves through Czechoslovakian border.
A truck with a few people on it was brought back.
The same truck brought their bodies to Cracow.
It-- it was-- life was not pleasant.
Life was not good.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] The first few days that I was in Poland, in Cracow, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to the places that I knew.
I looked at the building-- I couldn't walk in-- but I was born and raised.
I couldn't walk into that building.
And I said, yes.
And I told him, in Polish, [POLISH].
A lot of us survived.
That's what I told him.
It was not the-- the truth.
But I just had to tell him that.
He should not be so eager to rejoice that everybody was killed.
I did not go into that building.
But I saw my mother's curtains in the windows.
I couldn't bring myself to that.
And I saw other places, my friends' windows.
I looked around.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it was not pleasant.
I see an [POLISH] this is the police-- that Polish police-- standing talking to a girl.
And naturally I was reasonably recognized.
Because my hair was shaved twice in Auschwitz, once when I arrived, and once for punishment.
My cousins fell trying to defend that country on Silesia.
About a year-- a year and a few months.
Yeah.
I had no choice.
Because of the-- I got papers to come to America.
From my uncle.
Who was-- who was living in Vienna.
And his son was on the list of the Nazis, as a Communist.
And when they entered Vienna, on the 13th of March '38, two days later they knocked on my uncle's door for the son.
And his son knew, and he ran to Czechoslovakia [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in '38.
And then when they entered Czechoslovakia, he and another friend chartered a plane to go to Paris, to France.
There was a snowstorm.
And he died in this plane crash in the Alps.
We read about it in Poland, in 1938, the winter of 1938.
We read it.
But meanwhile, they didn't find the son, so they took the father.
And he was in prison in Vienna.
And after six months in prison, they sent him to a concentration camp near Bern.
I think-- I don't know what the rest is-- I don't know exactly which little camp it was.
But it was like a ghetto, where he tried to explain to me, my uncle.
Maybe it was the beginning of Theresienstadt, I don't know.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] being, my uncle-- my aunt was not the mother of this boy, of the fellow that they were looking for.
So they did not take her.
And meanwhile her family was-- came from Vienna to the United States.
And they were working for papers for the two of them to get them out of Austria.
And being she was free to come and go, she arranged the passports and she arranged everything, and he was released, being she proved that there is a passport and there is a visa for him.
So they allowed him to leave with the value of-- of $5 worth of thing-- of money, one and the other, nothing else they were allowed to take out.
And they left via Trieste in 1940, on the last boat from Trieste to the United States.
That was the one that sent the papers for me.
I met my husband in Paris in end of 1946, when I arrived to Paris.
My trip to United States.
Yes.
But he was not in concentration camps.
Yes.
He was Polish.
He was born in Nowy Sacz.
Morton Horowitz.
Mordecai. ?]
Mordecai, ?] yes.
Yes, by a rabbi.
That was in July of '48.
I stayed in Paris about two years and a couple months.
Yes.
Yes, as a-- as a seamstress, but-- as-- mainly by hand, finishing.
He was working as a printer.
That was his--
--profession in Poland.
That was his profession.
And that was his profession in the United States when--
Yes.
I had to keep on going to the [FRENCH].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because, you know, I was a [FRENCH], a stranger in the city, in the country.
And in order for me to work, I had to have papers in order.
And I had to go show them that I'm working, so they postpone the stay, whatever.
Because I-- I came in on a temporary stay.
Now, after a few months of having to go every month and losing a day's work, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and not, you know, I felt, OK, I'm going to do something.
One morning I woke up.
And I says, I'm going to take the courage.
It was a Saturday morning.
I found out where the [LAUGHS] office of the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] inner ministry is.
And I took my papers, and I went there.
We walk into the same building, the two of us.
And he shows me the door where I have to go.
And I-- and I told the secretary, whoever was sitting, the clerk, who I am, and that I'm trying to see someone who could help me out with that.
OK.
Wait a few minutes.
And she-- he said, that person that is here a few months and speaks French as you do, I will bend backwards and forwards for that person.
I could have stayed there.
I could have become a French citizen.
But I had chosen to be with relatives.
I had nobody there.
Mm-hmm.
I came to the United States April 1, 1949.
Mm-hmm.
I have two children.
Leah and Eli.
My daughter is a psychologist and lives in Minnesota.
My son is a CPA and lives in southern New York.
Paula.
He's married.
My daughter is single.
He is married.
Her name is Paula.
And they have a little boy.
His name is Marc.
Marc is three and a half years old, going on 30.
[LAUGHTER]
Yes.
I did wonder.
I did wonder.
I did wonder what did I do that maybe I was left to suffer.
Maybe I was left to mourn.
I don't know.
I had once a dream.
And [SOBBING] I never said it to anybody.
But way before-- that's true it happened in-- already in Tarnow, when we were still together, when we knew of the ghetto in-- in Cracow.
And we knew already that-- the killing of Ponary.
We heard of that.
We heard of Babi Yar.
We heard of a lot of problems of shipping people out of Warsaw ghetto.
We just did not know what happened.
But we heard of killing at Ponary and of killing at Babi Yar.
This we heard.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And I said, and somehow in a mysterious way, in an unknown way, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was all alone.
I was all alone.
And I explained to them I was in a valley, deep in a valley, and this-- with big fir trees reaching the sky.
Behind me was a building like a sukkah.
That was the only way I could describe it, like a sukkah that you build for the holiday Sukkot.
But it had windows, and it was painted green.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I smelled lilac that was May.
I smelled lilac.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And my father says to me, my god, what a dream.
But my dream came true exactly, to the trees and to the barrack, and to the month of May.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have sometimes premonitions, and I hate myself for it.
My husband died one month short of 48 years.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I hope he's in-- with peace now.
[SIGHS] It affected us all.
I try to be a good mother.
But like my daughter said lately, something-- we grew up in a home where there were always people, but no relatives.
[SOBBING] It had effect on my children.
I used to spend sometimes hours during the night standing by the baby's crib, listening if the baby's still alive.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] [SIGHS] The nightmares that I had, and that included my children, were real nightmares that I just couldn't come out of it.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] Life is very depressing.
And you have no one to speak to.
There is nobody you could talk to, nobody that could understand it.
It's true.
People that went through the same thing that I did, naturally if we get together, we have someone to talk to.
But raising children, you did not have time to run to your friends.
You had to be a mother.
You had to be a grandmother.
You had to be a grandfather.
You had to be a-- an uncle and an aunt, and everything together in one role, in one.
We had to do the spoiling.
We had to do the giving.
We had to be there constantly for our children.
They did not ask to be born.
They were a gift to us.
Yeah.
My mother had seven siblings-- no, excuse me, seven is a lie.
She had nine siblings.
They each have at least two children, husbands, wives.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They each had at least two, some of them had more.
Now, I have survivor cousin who is in Australia.
The aunt that emigrated to-- many years ago, before I was even born-- to America.
She had two children here.
And I have-- and I had an aunt in Israel, who emigrated way before I was born.
He was one of the pioneers in Petah Tikva.
